video_title: Why Hyperliquid Could Win the Everything Exchange Race
gist: Multicoin partner Tushar Jain argues the market at $63 is mispricing HYPE by treating Hyperliquid as merely a fast-growing perp DEX rather than the emerging backend for a global DeFi 'everything exchange.' He lays out Multicoin's bear/base/bull valuation framework (base case ~$319, >5x upside), the portfolio-margining and HIP-3 moats, why liquidations (not volume) reveal real traction, HYPE's clean buy-and-burn value capture, and a multi-year US regulatory onshoring path via the Clarity Act.

insights.claim: Multicoin believes the market at $63 is deeply mispricing HYPE by viewing Hyperliquid too narrowly as just a fast-growing perp DEX, when the real thesis is that it becomes the backend for a global DeFi 'everything exchange' where anyone anywhere can get exposure to any asset, event contract, or derivative.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: The bull case rests on re-rating Hyperliquid from a perp-DEX multiple to a platform/exchange multiple; watch whether non-perp markets and third-party frontends actually accrete to HYPE.
insights.quote: at $63 we believe the market is deeply mispricing hype viewing it too narrowly as just a fast growing perp

insights.claim: Jain's core conviction is on the destination (a DeFi 'everything exchange' with localized centralized frontends over a global DeFi liquidity backend), not the specific path, citing that certainty about both goal and path is a classic investing mistake and that Hyperliquid is currently the most credible path.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Frames HYPE as a bet on an inevitable end-state, meaning the position should be revisited if a different venue becomes the more credible backend rather than held dogmatically.
insights.quote: some of the worst investing mistakes you can make is say I am certain about the goal and I'm certain about the path to get there and then you're wrong

insights.claim: Jain uses a ride-sharing analogy to justify path-agnostic conviction: being right that ride-sharing would win but betting on the wrong company (or wrong regulatory path like taxi medallions) makes no money, so he holds conviction only on the 'everything exchange' destination.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Signals Multicoin would rotate away from Hyperliquid if execution falters, so the thesis is conditional rather than a permanent HYPE hold.

insights.claim: Jain expects a permanent market structure of localized, regulated, centralized frontends (handling user acquisition, regulators, and support) sitting atop a single global DeFi liquidity backend, and is investing on how to reach that state rather than betting on any one frontend.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Implies the durable value accrues to the liquidity backend, so investors should watch which backend wins aggregated frontend routing, not which app has the users.

insights.claim: Jain argues Robinhood is a US-focused brokerage/super-app rather than an exchange and could plausibly route its volumes to Hyperliquid on the backend once regulatory clarity arrives, keeping the customer relationship while sending liquidity to the most liquid venue.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: A Robinhood-to-Hyperliquid routing deal would be a major distribution catalyst; regulatory clarity is the gating factor to watch.

themes.theme: Portfolio Margining as the Compounding Moat

insights.claim: Jain's single most exciting reason to own HYPE is portfolio (cross) margining across product categories, letting a trader hold Bitcoin, ETH, oil, gold, event contracts (e.g. Fed rate decisions), and options against one unified collateral account that nets P&L automatically.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Capital efficiency from unified collateral is the differentiator; it is the feature to track for whether Hyperliquid out-retains capital versus rivals launching identical markets.
insights.quote: portfolio margining is the most exciting because it empowers all of the other categories of products

insights.claim: Jain concedes competitors can copy any individual market or product, but argues portfolio margining plus existing on-platform balances enables cross-selling the next product with no new deposit action, giving Hyperliquid compounding returns to scale that a new entrant cannot easily match.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Reframes the moat as switching-cost / installed-capital economics rather than product novelty; incumbency of collateral, not features, is the defensible edge.

insights.claim: Jain highlights that cross-margining across different product categories (perps plus event contracts plus options plus tokenized commodities) has not yet been seen on existing perp DEXes, enabling paired trades like long Bitcoin hedged with a Fed-rate event contract, or a DRAM-price bet paired against Micron stock.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Novel cross-category structured trades could pull in sophisticated flow; first-mover on unified multi-asset margin is the specific edge to monitor.
insights.quote: cross margin between these different categories of products I don't believe we've seen

insights.claim: HIP-3 decentralizes market creation on Hyperliquid: deployers must post a bond of 500,000 HYPE (permissionlessly, some raised from the community) to launch their own markets and keep half the trading fees, which is how Hyperliquid gained deep oil and gold markets on demand.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: The 500k HYPE bond is a structural token sink and a supply-side flywheel; rising deployer count directly locks HYPE and expands the market surface.
insights.quote: They have to post a bond of 500k hype in order to have the ability to deploy markets, but that's permissionless

insights.claim: HIP-3 volume went from basically nothing to roughly a third of total Hyperliquid volume within months, and Jain predicts HIP-3 plus HIP-4 will eventually be the vast majority of Hyperliquid volume because there will simply be far more markets.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Rapid HIP-3 volume ramp is the leading indicator that the platformization thesis is playing out; track the HIP-3 share of total volume as a KPI.
insights.quote: HIP3 volumes went from basically nothing to I believe about a third of total volume on Hyperlid in the matter of months

insights.claim: Jain frames HIP-3 (supply-side market creation) and builder codes (demand-side frontends that route flow for a fee share) as two sides of the same decentralization, letting Hyperliquid position itself as a credibly neutral hub that turns would-be competitors into aligned collaborators.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Wallets like Phantom, MetaMask and Infinex routing to Hyperliquid entrench it; a competitor-to-collaborator dynamic is the network-effect story to watch.
insights.quote: it has the potential to turn a competitor into a collaborator

insights.claim: Trade XYZ is the dominant HIP-3 deployer on Hyperliquid, a brand-new Hyperliquid-native company making substantial revenue on its own while remaining highly aligned with Hyperliquid rather than competing.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Trade XYZ is a proof point that third parties can build profitable businesses on Hyperliquid rails; its growth is a concrete gauge of platform adoption.

insights.claim: Jain says the biggest bullish signal he still wants but does not yet see is a major incumbent like Coinbase using builder codes with Hyperliquid as the backend, whereas Coinbase is currently choosing to build its own first-party perp exchange and compete.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: A Coinbase-routes-to-Hyperliquid moment would be the key confirming catalyst; its absence is the current gap in the bull case.
insights.quote: the biggest signal I would want to see as a hype bull is somebody like Coinbase using builder codes and having Hyperlid as the backend

insights.claim: Jain contends non-custodial wallets (Phantom, MetaMask) will integrate Hyperliquid before centralized exchanges because wallets are in the business of routing users to the best execution, while Coinbase keeps trades on its own platform at what he calls egregious fees even when a better price exists elsewhere.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Incentive alignment predicts the integration order; wallet integrations are the near-term adoption vector, CEX routing the longer-term prize.

insights.claim: Jain says a major catalyst that would make him even more bullish is a regulated, custodial frontend plugging into Hyperliquid's backend liquidity, which no player has yet done because current integrations (wallets) work only by being non-custodial software.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: A regulated custodial frontend routing to Hyperliquid would unlock institutional and retail-regulated flow; it is a named future catalyst to watch for.

insights.claim: Jain dismisses trading volume as the easiest metric to fake (two wash-trading accounts can generate infinite volume at the same price) and instead ranks liquidations as the truest signal of real users taking directional risk, because liquidations carry an unfakeable penalty that means someone actually lost money.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Gives investors a diligence hierarchy—net fees, open interest, then liquidations—for cutting through incentivized volume when comparing perp venues.
insights.quote: Liquidations explicitly carry a liquidation penalty that cannot be faked

insights.claim: Jain warns that open interest is now also being gamed, citing Lighter specifically for subsidizing funding-fee rebates that incentivize wash-hedged accounts to inflate OI without taking real risk, which is why he escalates to liquidation data as the cleanest signal.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Flags that OI comparisons across DEXes can be manipulated via funding subsidies; adjust competitor comparisons for incentive programs before trusting OI league tables.

insights.claim: Hyperliquid has a much higher ratio of liquidations to trading volume than its main competitors Aster and Lighter, which Jain reads as evidence of genuine directional risk-taking versus incentivized/farmed activity, with Coin Glass cited as a public data source.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: The liquidation-to-volume ratio on Coin Glass is a concrete, verifiable metric to independently test the 'real users' claim against Aster and Lighter.

insights.claim: The host notes Lighter trades at roughly a 6.5x revenue multiple versus Hyperliquid at ~18x (about 3x richer); Jain declines to endorse the multiple, arguing valuation multiples reflect the market's view of revenue durability and must be compared on an apples-to-apples trailing-12-month basis.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The premium implies the market already prices Hyperliquid's revenue as more durable; the multiple gap is the key relative-value question for a Lighter-vs-Hyperliquid pair trade.
insights.quote: lighter is trading at a 6.5 x premium on its revenue where Hyperlid is trading as at an 18x premium on its revenue

insights.claim: Multicoin's base case applies a 20x multiple to $8 billion in earnings for a $160 billion valuation, which on an adjusted supply of 502 million tokens implies a HYPE price above $319, more than 5x upside from the ~$63 level.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Anchors the central price target and the earnings/multiple assumptions behind it; the $8B earnings figure is the load-bearing input to stress-test.
insights.quote: at a 20x multiple on $8 billion in earnings in our bare case we arrive at a $160 billion valuation... implies a hyped price of over $319 representing more than a 5x upside

insights.claim: Jain stresses the valuation is a framework presenting three illustrative scenarios (bear, base, bull) drawn from many more internal cases, not a precise point forecast, and urges readers to substitute their own assumptions rather than take any number at face value.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Signals the price targets are scenario ranges, not commitments; treat the assumption inputs, not the headline $319, as the analytical product.

insights.claim: The valuation rests on four critical assumptions: total crypto derivatives volume growth, the DEX share of that derivatives market, Hyperliquid's share of the DEX segment, and the ratio of stablecoins on Hyperliquid to trading volume (which drives Coinbase-deal interest income).
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Gives the exact four levers to model; each is independently checkable, letting an investor build their own bull/bear scenario.

insights.claim: Total crypto derivatives volume has grown ~45% annualized over the past five years; Multicoin's base case assumes 35% forward growth, the bear case only 10%, and the bull case 50%.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: The volume-growth assumption is the first compounding lever; a slowdown toward the 10% bear figure would materially compress the target.

insights.claim: DEXes were ~0% of crypto derivatives volume in 2022 and are now ~16%; Multicoin's base case extrapolates to 32%, the bear case to 20%, and the bull case to derivatives DEXes reaching half the total crypto derivatives market.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: The DEX-share migration from 16% is the second compounding lever; tracking the CEX-to-DEX derivatives shift validates or breaks the thesis.
insights.quote: DEX's made up basically 0% of derivatives volume in 2022 and are now up to 16%

insights.claim: Multicoin's base case holds Hyperliquid's share of DeFi derivatives constant at 30%, which Jain calls conservative because Hyperliquid is currently at ~59% of open interest (lower on volume due to competitors' farming), and he expects that share to actually grow as farming behavior proves unsustainable.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: The gap between the 30% modeled share and current ~59% OI share is the built-in cushion in the base case; a slide below 30% would be the bear trigger.
insights.quote: Hyperliquid is currently at almost 60% of open interest. It's 59% of open interest

themes.theme: Value Capture, Coinbase Deal & Token Unlocks

insights.claim: Under the Coinbase deal Hyperliquid earns 90% of the interest on its stablecoin (USDC) balances, and that yield flows to buy back the HYPE token; Multicoin assumes the stablecoin-to-open-interest-to-volume ratio stays constant, so stablecoin balances (and buyback fuel) scale linearly with volume.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: The 90% interest capture converts idle collateral into a direct HYPE buyback stream; rising stablecoin balances are a mechanical bid on the token.
insights.quote: hyperlid is getting 90% of the interest on that stable coin balance

insights.claim: Jain calls HYPE's value-capture the cleanest in crypto: no separate equity entity, revenue buys and burns HYPE, HIP-3 deployers must stake HYPE, and HyperEVM priority fees are paid in HYPE, so every activity aligns around one asset with no ambiguity about token-holder participation.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: The absence of an equity leakage layer means fundamentals flow directly to the token; this is the crux of why Multicoin favors HYPE over governance-token structures.
insights.quote: hype I think as a token story is probably the greatest story that cryptos had in terms of direct relationship between value creation and value capture

insights.claim: Core contributors were allocated ~24% of total HYPE supply vesting at roughly 10 million HYPE per month through 2028, which at $63 equals about $625 million per month unlocking, a risk the host flags and Jain acknowledges but downplays.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The ~$625M/month potential sell pressure is the primary supply overhang; net buyback flows must outpace it to sustain price.
insights.quote: core contributors were allocated about 24% of the total supply... vests at around 10 million hype per month through 2028

insights.claim: Jain's team-durability check is behavioral: with ~14 engineers already at generational wealth roughly 18 months in, a rug-pull-style abandonment would already have shown up, but instead the pace of execution continues unabated, which he treats as the key signal that the team is mission- not money-motivated.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Reframes key-man/retention risk as an observable execution-pace question; a visible slowdown in shipping would be the real warning sign for HYPE holders.

insights.claim: Jain emphasizes Hyperliquid built a ~$60 billion fully-diluted platform with only about 14 engineers, positioning it as a lean, AI-age company where the perp contract type is attractive but explicitly not the core competitive edge since anyone (Coinbase, Binance, Kalshi) can launch perps.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Directs the moat analysis away from the perp instrument (commoditized) toward execution speed and platform economics; small headcount is the operating-leverage story.
insights.quote: The contract type is really attractive but that's not a core part of the thesis

insights.claim: Jain lays out a multi-year, four-step onshoring path for Hyperliquid: US perp legalization (started with the Kalshi CFTC approval), passage of the Clarity Act with DeFi safe harbors, Hyperliquid meeting its requirements (likely open-source code and validator count), and a compliant regulated US frontend routing volume to the DeFi backend.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Gives a concrete legislative checklist to track US onshoring; Clarity Act passage and its final safe-harbor text are the pivotal milestones.

insights.claim: Jain cautions that crypto markets wildly overestimate regulatory speed, citing that the GENIUS bill passed last year but only takes effect January 2027 after rulemaking and comment periods, so Hyperliquid onshoring will take years, not the pace crypto expects.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Tempers expectations for a fast US catalyst; positions the onshoring thesis as a multi-year option, not a near-term re-rating trigger.
insights.quote: The Genius bill was passed last year. It's still not in effect. It goes into effect January 2027

insights.claim: Jain argues Hyperliquid may not need to take explicit action to come onshore because builder codes let compliant frontends permissionlessly aggregate US users and route volume, so the core team only needs to open-source code and grow the validator set, which he expects them to do anyway to protect against a future hostile administration.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Suggests onshoring can happen via third-party frontends without core-team registration; watch open-sourcing and validator-count expansion as the enabling technical steps.

insights.claim: Jain notes the CME is suing the CFTC over its approval of onshore perps, which he reads as an incumbent trying to protect a monopoly and therefore a strong product-market-fit signal that perps have captured attention beyond crypto.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Incumbent legal resistance is framed as a bullish tell for perps as an instrument; the CME v. CFTC suit is a catalyst to monitor for US perp legality.
insights.quote: you saw this announcement from the CME that they're suing the CFTC for approving onshore per

insights.claim: Jain observes Hyperliquid is the most offshore of the perp platforms (Coinbase, Robinhood, Kalshi, and Lighter are onshore or US-registered), and while that offshore status grants freedom and flexibility today, it leaves him unsure of Hyperliquid's ability to penetrate the US market without the regulatory path playing out.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Offshore status is a double-edged sword; US market access is the key uncertainty separating Hyperliquid from onshore rivals like Lighter.

insights.claim: Jain dismisses Hyperliquid's lack of direct fiat on-ramps as a non-issue, arguing stablecoin proliferation makes stablecoin on-ramps sufficient, and citing Multicoin portfolio company fun.xyz as already solving instant fiat/stablecoin onboarding, so on-ramps are not an edge for incumbents like Coinbase.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Counters the bear point that Coinbase's banking relationships and toll-taking on-ramp position are defensible; watch stablecoin on-ramp adoption as the disintermediating force.

insights.claim: Jain frames a huge secular tailwind of financialization: as AI displaces labor, he expects more human attention and energy to shift from labor markets to financial markets, driving structural growth in trading and investing that benefits Hyperliquid and peers.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: A macro demand narrative for exchange volume broadly; positions HYPE as a levered bet on the financialization-of-attention trend, not just crypto share gains.
insights.quote: as we move to a world of AI, I expect that more and more human attention and energy will be put towards financial markets as opposed to labor markets

insights.claim: Jain positions Coinbase as Hyperliquid's most direct competitor but argues the decisive trade-off is decentralization, permissionless access, and censorship resistance, which DeFi offers and centralized finance cannot, so the bull case ultimately requires believing in those crypto ideals.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Defines the axis on which Hyperliquid must beat Coinbase; the thesis is partly ideological, so mainstream adoption of permissionless venues is the swing variable.

insights.claim: Jain acknowledges the perp instrument itself has low penetration across broader traditional finance and is a strong growth vector, but repeatedly stresses it is not Hyperliquid's competitive edge since Kalshi, Coinbase, and Binance all offer or can offer perps.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Separates the 'buy perps as a growth instrument' trade (many ways to express it) from the 'buy Hyperliquid specifically' trade (platform economics), sharpening why to pick HYPE.

insights.claim: When asked directly why Multicoin co-founder Kyle Samani reportedly dislikes Hyperliquid, Jain declined to speak for him, leaving an unresolved internal-disagreement signal at a firm that is otherwise publicly bullish on HYPE.
insights.speaker: Tushar Jain (guest, Multicoin partner)
insights.so_what: Hints that conviction on HYPE is not unanimous even inside Multicoin; a notable caveat to weigh against the firm's published bull thesis.
